title: DNA sequence Trie
description: Store and search DNA sequences using Trie

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize storage with empty list. Step 2: Insert 'ACGT' -> sequences = ['ACGT']. Step 3: Insert 'TGCA' -> sequences = ['ACGT', 'TGCA']. Step 4: Search for 'ACGT'. Step 5: Compare with first sequence 'ACGT': lengths match (4==4). Compare characters: index0: A==A, index1: C==C, index2: G==G, index3: T==T. All match, return True.

Step 1: Initialize storage. Step 2: Insert 'AAA' -> sequences = ['AAA']. Step 3: Insert 'CCC' -> sequences = ['AAA', 'CCC']. Step 4: Search for 'GGG'. Step 5: Compare with 'AAA': lengths match (3==3). Compare characters: index0: A!=G, mismatch, move to next sequence. Step 6: Compare with 'CCC': lengths match. index0: C!=G, mismatch. Step 7: No more sequences, return False.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Store DNA sequences in a list and search by iterating through each sequence to compare characters. Use this for small datasets or when simplicity is prioritized over performance.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m) for search, where n is number of sequences and m is average length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DNA Sequence Storage and Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of storing DNA sequences in a list and searching by comparing each sequence character-by-character.
ladders.explanation: This approach implements a brute-force solution for storing and searching DNA sequences. The intuition is straightforward: maintain a list of all sequences, and when searching for a pattern, compare it against every stored sequence. This works because DNA sequences are strings over the alphabet {A, C, G, T}, and string comparison is deterministic. The algorithm has two main operations: 1) Insert: Add a new DNA sequence to the list. 2) Search: Check if a given pattern exists in any stored sequence by iterating through the list and comparing characters. For each sequence in the list, compare it character-by-character with the search pattern. If they match completely, return True. If no sequence matches, return False. Time complexity: O(n * m) for search, where n is the number of sequences and m is the average length of sequences. Space complexity: O(n * m) to store all sequences. Connection to L1: This full problem uses the key subroutine of sequence comparison (L1) as its core operation. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty sequences correctly. 2) Forgetting to check all sequences before returning False. 3) Incorrectly comparing sequences of different lengths. Avoid these by: 1) Adding edge case checks. 2) Using a flag to track matches. 3) Comparing lengths first. Real code pattern: class DNAStorage: def __init__(self): self.sequences = [] def insert(self, seq): self.sequences.append(seq) def search(self, pattern): for seq in self.sequences: if len(seq) != len(pattern): continue match = True for i in range(len(pattern)): if seq[i] != pattern[i]: match = False break if match: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize storage with empty list. Step 2: Insert 'ACGT' -> sequences = ['ACGT']. Step 3: Insert 'TGCA' -> sequences = ['ACGT', 'TGCA']. Step 4: Search for 'ACGT'. Step 5: Compare with first sequence 'ACGT': lengths match (4==4). Compare characters: index0: A==A, index1: C==C, index2: G==G, index3: T==T. All match, return True.

Step 1: Initialize storage. Step 2: Insert 'AAA' -> sequences = ['AAA']. Step 3: Insert 'CCC' -> sequences = ['AAA', 'CCC']. Step 4: Search for 'GGG'. Step 5: Compare with 'AAA': lengths match (3==3). Compare characters: index0: A!=G, mismatch, move to next sequence. Step 6: Compare with 'CCC': lengths match. index0: C!=G, mismatch. Step 7: No more sequences, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sequence Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two DNA sequences character-by-character to check if they are identical.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute-force search. The intuition is that two sequences are equal only if they have the same length and every corresponding character matches. This works because DNA sequences are ordered strings, and equality is defined by exact character alignment. The algorithm: 1) Check if the lengths of the two sequences are equal. If not, return False immediately. 2) Iterate through each index from 0 to length-1. 3) Compare characters at the current index. If any mismatch, return False. 4) If all characters match, return True. Time complexity: O(m) where m is the length of the sequences. Space complexity: O(1) as only a few variables are used. Connection to L0: This comparison is used in the search function of L0 to check each stored sequence against the search pattern. Common mistakes: 1) Not checking length first, leading to index errors. 2) Using 'break' instead of 'return False' when a mismatch is found. 3) Forgetting to handle empty sequences. Avoid by: 1) Always compare lengths upfront. 2) Return immediately on mismatch. 3) Treat empty sequences as valid. Real code pattern: def compare_sequences(seq1, seq2): if len(seq1) != len(seq2): return False for i in range(len(seq1)): if seq1[i] != seq2[i]: return False return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Check lengths: len(seq1)=4, len(seq2)=4, equal. Step 2: i=0: seq1[0]='A', seq2[0]='A', match. Step 3: i=1: 'C'=='C', match. Step 4: i=2: 'G'=='G', match. Step 5: i=3: 'T'=='T', match. Step 6: All characters match, return True.
Step 1: Check lengths: both 4, equal. Step 2: i=0: seq1[0]='A', seq2[0]='T', mismatch. Step 3: Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative List Search
ladders.desc: Search for an item in a list by checking each element one by one.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements linear search through a list. The intuition is that to find if an item exists in a collection, you can examine each element sequentially until you find a match or exhaust the list. This works because lists maintain order, and checking each element is guaranteed to find the item if it exists. The algorithm: 1) Start from the first element of the list. 2) For each element, check if it matches the target. 3) If a match is found, return True (or the index). 4) If the end of the list is reached without a match, return False (or -1). Time complexity: O(n) where n is the number of elements. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L1: In L0, this logic is used to iterate through stored sequences, and L1's comparison checks each sequence against the target. Common mistakes: 1) Not handling empty lists. 2) Returning too early on the first non-match. 3) Using the wrong comparison operator. Avoid by: 1) Check if list is empty first. 2) Only return when a match is found; otherwise continue. 3) Use '==' for equality checks. Real code pattern: def linear_search(items, target): for item in items: if item == target: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with first item 'ACGT'. Step 2: Compare 'ACGT' == 'ACGT'? Yes, match found. Step 3: Return True immediately.
Step 1: First item 'AAA': 'AAA' == 'GGG'? No. Step 2: Next item 'CCC': 'CCC' == 'GGG'? No. Step 3: No more items, return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access individual characters in a string and compare them for equality.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves working with strings at the character level. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and you can access each character by its position (index) to perform operations like comparison. This works because strings in most programming languages are indexed starting from 0, and characters can be compared using equality operators. The algorithm for comparing two strings character-by-character: 1) Ensure both strings have the same length (if comparing for equality). 2) Use a loop to iterate from index 0 to length-1. 3) At each index, compare the characters using '=='. 4) If all characters match, the strings are equal. Time complexity: O(m) for strings of length m. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in L1 to compare sequences, which is then used in L2's list search. Common mistakes: 1) Index out of bounds when strings have different lengths. 2) Comparing strings directly without indexing in a loop (which is fine for equality but not for character-by-character analysis). 3) Using assignment '=' instead of comparison '=='. Avoid by: 1) Check lengths first. 2) Use explicit indexing when needed. 3) Double-check operators. Real code pattern: str1 = 'ACGT'; str2 = 'ACGT'; if len(str1) != len(str2): return False; for i in range(len(str1)): if str1[i] != str2[i]: return False; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Check lengths: both length 1, equal. Step 2: i=0: str1[0]='A', str2[0]='A'. Step 3: Compare: 'A' == 'A' is True. Step 4: All characters match, return True.
Step 1: Check lengths: both length 2, equal. Step 2: i=0: str1[0]='A', str2[0]='C'. Step 3: Compare: 'A' == 'C' is False. Step 4: Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through Indices
ladders.desc: Use a for-loop to iterate over a range of numbers representing indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a loop to repeat an action for each index in a sequence. The intuition is that many operations on sequences (like strings or lists) require performing the same task for each position, and a loop allows this without writing repetitive code. This works because loops in programming execute a block of code multiple times, with a control variable (like 'i') taking on different values each time. The algorithm: 1) Determine the number of iterations (e.g., length of a string). 2) Set up a for-loop that runs from 0 to length-1. 3) Inside the loop, use the index variable to access elements. Time complexity: O(n) for n iterations. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: This looping is used in L3 to iterate through string indices for character comparison. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (e.g., using 'range(len+1)' instead of 'range(len)'). 2) Not updating the index variable correctly. 3) Infinite loops with while-loops. Avoid by: 1) Remember that indexing starts at 0. 2) Use for-loops with range for simplicity. 3) Test with small examples. Real code pattern: for i in range(5): print(i) # prints 0,1,2,3,4

examples.trace: Step 1: Set up loop: for i in range(4). Step 2: i=0: print 0. Step 3: i=1: print 1. Step 4: i=2: print 2. Step 5: i=3: print 3. Step 6: Loop ends.
Step 1: String length = 2. Step 2: for i in range(2). Step 3: i=0: access 'A'. Step 4: i=1: access 'C'. Step 5: Loop ends.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Strings and Characters in Programming
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences of characters and basic string operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of variables and data types, which are essential for any programming task. The intuition is that variables are named containers that hold data, and data types define what kind of data (e.g., text, numbers) can be stored. This works because programming languages use variables to store information temporarily during execution, allowing operations on that data. Key concepts: 1) Variables: Declare with a name and assign a value, e.g., seq = 'ACGT'. 2) Data types: Strings for text (e.g., 'A'), integers for whole numbers (e.g., 4), booleans for True/False. 3) Operations: Assignment (=), comparison (==), access length with len(). Time and space analysis are not applicable at this basic level. Connection to L4: Variables store the strings and indices that L4 loops through. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==). 2) Using wrong data types (e.g., adding strings as numbers). 3) Not initializing variables before use. Avoid by: 1) Practice with simple examples. 2) Check data types with type(). 3) Initialize variables with default values. Real code pattern: dna = 'ACGT' # string variable; length = len(dna) # integer variable; is_match = (dna == 'ACGT') # boolean variable

examples.input: Store string 'A' in a variable and check its length
examples.trace: Step 1: Declare variable: seq = 'A'. Step 2: seq now holds the string 'A'. Step 3: Compute length: len(seq) = 1. Step 4: Store in another variable: length = 1.
Step 1: Declare seq1 = 'AC'. Step 2: Declare seq2 = 'CA'. Step 3: Compare: seq1 == seq2 evaluates to 'AC' == 'CA', which is False. Step 4: Store result: match = False.

approaches.name: Trie with Array Children O(L)
approaches.summary: Implement a Trie where each node has an array of size 4 (for A, C, G, T) to store child pointers, enabling efficient prefix-based storage and search. This is the standard approach for DNA sequences due to the fixed alphabet.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) for insert and search, where L is sequence length
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N * L * 4) in worst case, but optimized for DNA's small alphabet

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete DNA Sequence Trie Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement a Trie data structure with array children to store and search DNA sequences efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires building a Trie (prefix tree) specifically optimized for DNA sequences, which consist of only four characters: A, C, G, T. The intuition is that a Trie allows us to store sequences in a way that shares common prefixes, making prefix-based searches extremely efficient. Each node in the Trie represents a character in a sequence and has an array of size 4 for child pointers (indexed 0-3 corresponding to A, C, G, T). This fixed-size array eliminates the overhead of hash maps or linked lists, leveraging DNA's small alphabet for optimal performance. The step-by-step algorithm involves: 1) Define a TrieNode class with an array children[4] and a boolean is_end flag. 2) For insertion, traverse the Trie according to each character in the sequence, creating new nodes as needed, and mark the last node as is_end = True. 3) For search, traverse the Trie; if we reach the end of the sequence and the last node has is_end = True, the sequence exists. Time complexity is O(L) for both operations, where L is sequence length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(N * L * 4) in the worst case if no prefixes are shared, but typically much less due to prefix sharing. Connection to higher levels: This integrates all sub-routines into a cohesive solution. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing of characters (e.g., mapping A to index 0, C to 1, etc.), forgetting to set is_end, or not handling empty sequences. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None] * 4; self.is_end = False; class DNATrie: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def _char_to_index(self, ch): mapping = {'A':0, 'C':1, 'G':2, 'T':3}; return mapping[ch]; def insert(self, sequence): node = self.root; for ch in sequence: idx = self._char_to_index(ch); if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True; def search(self, sequence): node = self.root; for ch in sequence: idx = self._char_to_index(ch); if not node.children[idx]: return False; node = node.children[idx]; return node.is_end;

examples.trace: 1. Initialize empty Trie with root node. 2. Insert 'ACGT': Start at root. For 'A' (idx 0): root.children[0] is None, create new node A. Move to node A. For 'C' (idx 1): A.children[1] is None, create new node C. Move to node C. For 'G' (idx 2): C.children[2] is None, create new node G. Move to node G. For 'T' (idx 3): G.children[3] is None, create new node T. Move to node T. Set T.is_end = True. 3. Search 'ACGT': Start at root. For 'A' (idx 0): root.children[0] exists (node A), move to A. For 'C' (idx 1): A.children[1] exists (node C), move to C. For 'G' (idx 2): C.children[2] exists (node G), move to G. For 'T' (idx 3): G.children[3] exists (node T), move to T. Check T.is_end = True, return True.

1. Initialize empty Trie. 2. Insert 'AC': Root -> A (idx 0) -> C (idx 1), set C.is_end = True. 3. Insert 'ACT': Root -> A -> C -> T (idx 3), set T.is_end = True (C.is_end remains True). 4. Search 'AC': Root -> A -> C, C.is_end = True, return True. 5. Search 'ACG': Root -> A -> C -> G (idx 2), but C.children[2] is None (since only T child exists), return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Function for DNA Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the insert operation to add a DNA sequence into the Trie.
ladders.explanation: The insert function is crucial as it builds the Trie structure by adding sequences. The intuition is to traverse the Trie from the root, following the path defined by each character in the sequence. If a node for a character doesn't exist, we create it, ensuring the Trie expands only as needed. This efficient insertion leverages the fixed alphabet of DNA by using an array of size 4 for children, allowing O(1) access per character. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Start at the root node. 2) For each character in the sequence: a) Map the character to an index (0 for A, 1 for C, 2 for G, 3 for T). b) Check if the child at that index exists. c) If not, create a new TrieNode. d) Move to that child node. 3) After processing all characters, mark the current node as the end of a sequence (set is_end = True). Time complexity is O(L), where L is sequence length, as we perform constant-time operations per character. Space complexity is O(L) for the new nodes created if no prefixes are shared. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is a core component of the full Trie implementation; without it, sequences cannot be stored. Common mistakes include not creating nodes for all characters, forgetting to set is_end, or incorrect character indexing. Real code pattern: def insert(self, sequence): node = self.root; for ch in sequence: idx = self._char_to_index(ch); if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True;

examples.output: Trie structure with nodes for C, A, T
examples.trace: 1. Start at root. 2. For 'C' (idx 1): root.children[1] is None, create new node C. Move to node C. 3. For 'A' (idx 0): C.children[0] is None, create new node A. Move to node A. 4. For 'T' (idx 3): A.children[3] is None, create new node T. Move to node T. 5. Set T.is_end = True. Result: root -> C -> A -> T (is_end=True).

1. Assume Trie has 'G' inserted: root -> G (is_end=True). 2. Insert 'GA': Start at root. For 'G' (idx 2): root.children[2] exists (node G), move to G. For 'A' (idx 0): G.children[0] is None, create new node A. Move to node A. Set A.is_end = True. Result: root -> G (is_end=True) -> A (is_end=True).

testCases.expected: No change (or handle as error, but typically ignore)
Root has child A with is_end=True
Shared path root->A->C with C.is_end=True, and C->T with T.is_end=True
Path of length 7 with last node.is_end=True
Root->A with two children T and G
Root->T with T.is_end=True and T->A with A.is_end=True

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character Index Mapping and Trie Traversal
ladders.desc: Map DNA characters to array indices and traverse the Trie nodes accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves converting DNA characters (A, C, G, T) to indices (0, 1, 2, 3) to access the children array in Trie nodes, enabling efficient traversal. The intuition is that DNA's fixed alphabet of four characters allows us to use a simple array instead of a hash map, reducing overhead and providing O(1) access time. By mapping each character to a unique index, we can quickly navigate the Trie during insert and search operations. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Define a helper function _char_to_index(ch) that returns 0 for 'A', 1 for 'C', 2 for 'G', 3 for 'T'. 2) For traversal (e.g., in insert or search): a) Start at the root node. b) For each character in the sequence, compute its index using _char_to_index. c) Access node.children[idx] to get the child node. d) If performing insert and child is None, create a new node. e) Move to the child node. Time complexity per character is O(1) for the mapping and array access. Space complexity is O(1) for the mapping table. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded within the insert function to navigate the Trie. Common mistakes include incorrect mapping (e.g., swapping indices), not handling invalid characters (though DNA sequences are assumed valid), or using a slow mapping method. Real code pattern: def _char_to_index(self, ch): mapping = {'A':0, 'C':1, 'G':2, 'T':3}; return mapping[ch]; Then in traversal: idx = self._char_to_index(ch); child = node.children[idx];

examples.trace: 1. For 'A': Use mapping {'A':0, 'C':1, 'G':2, 'T':3}, return 0. 2. For 'C': Return 1. 3. For 'G': Return 2. 4. For 'T': Return 3.

examples.input: Traverse root for sequence 'GT' assuming root has children
examples.output: Move to node G, then node T
examples.trace: 1. Start at root. 2. For 'G': idx = mapping['G'] = 2. Access root.children[2] (assume exists, node G). Move to node G. 3. For 'T': idx = mapping['T'] = 3. Access G.children[3] (assume exists, node T). Move to node T.

ladders.title: Building Block — TrieNode Structure with Array Children
ladders.desc: Define the TrieNode class with an array of size 4 for children and an end-of-sequence flag.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating the fundamental node structure for the Trie. The intuition is that each node in the Trie represents a character in a DNA sequence and needs to store pointers to its children (possible next characters) and a flag indicating if it marks the end of a stored sequence. By using an array of size 4, we leverage DNA's small alphabet (A, C, G, T) for efficient, direct access to children without the overhead of dynamic data structures. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Define a class TrieNode. 2) In the constructor, initialize an array children of size 4 with all elements set to None (or null). This array will hold references to child TrieNodes. 3) Also initialize a boolean variable is_end to False, indicating this node does not necessarily mark the end of a sequence. Time complexity for node creation is O(1). Space complexity per node is O(4) = O(1) for the array, plus O(1) for the flag. Connection to L2: This node structure is used in traversal, where we access children via indices. Common mistakes include using a list of wrong size, not initializing is_end, or using a dictionary instead of an array for performance. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None] * 4; self.is_end = False;

examples.trace: 1. Call TrieNode constructor. 2. Initialize children as a list of 4 None values. 3. Set is_end to False.

examples.input: Create a TrieNode and set is_end to True
examples.trace: 1. Create TrieNode as above. 2. Set node.is_end = True.

testCases.input: Create TrieNode, check is_end initial value
Create TrieNode, set children[0] to new TrieNode
Create TrieNode, set is_end to True
Create two TrieNodes, link as parent and child
Create TrieNode, check children[3] initial value
Create TrieNode, set children[2] to None
Create TrieNode, verify all children are None
Create TrieNode, set is_end to False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access and Update in Python
ladders.desc: Perform basic operations on a fixed-size array: access by index and update values.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with arrays (lists in Python) to store and retrieve elements at specific indices. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access time, making them ideal for the children pointers in a Trie node. For DNA sequences, we use an array of size 4, where each index corresponds to a character (0:A, 1:C, 2:G, 3:T). Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an array of size 4 with default values (e.g., None). In Python: children = [None] * 4. 2) To access an element at index i: value = children[i]. This retrieves the child node pointer. 3) To update an element at index i: children[i] = new_value. This sets or changes the child node pointer. Time complexity for both access and update is O(1). Space complexity is O(4) = O(1) for the array. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the TrieNode to manage child pointers. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (e.g., index 4 in a size-4 array), not initializing the array properly, or confusing list operations. Real code pattern: children = [None] * 4; # Initialize; value = children[0]; # Access; children[1] = TrieNode(); # Update.

examples.input: Initialize array of size 4 with None, access index 2
examples.trace: 1. Create array: children = [None] * 4 -> [None, None, None, None]. 2. Access children[2]: Returns None.

examples.trace: 1. Start with array [None, None, None, None]. 2. Update: children[0] = 5. Array becomes [5, None, None, None]. 3. Access children[0]: Returns 5.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — DNA Alphabet and Character Representation
ladders.desc: Understand the DNA alphabet (A, C, G, T) and how characters are represented in programming.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic building block of DNA sequences: the four nucleotide bases represented by characters A (adenine), C (cytosine), G (guanine), and T (thymine). The intuition is that DNA sequences are strings composed solely of these four characters, making them ideal for optimization in data structures like Tries. In programming, characters are typically represented as strings or chars, and we can map them to numerical indices for efficient processing. Step-by-step explanation: 1) DNA sequences are ordered lists of these bases, e.g., 'ACGT' is a sequence. 2) In code, we treat them as strings: sequence = 'ACGT'. 3) To work with them in a Trie, we often convert each character to an index (0-3) for array access. This mapping is straightforward due to the fixed size. Time and space analysis: Accessing a character in a string is O(1), and mapping can be O(1) with a dictionary or array. Connection to L4: This concept underlies why we use an array of size 4 in the Trie node. Common mistakes include assuming other characters are valid or not handling case sensitivity (DNA sequences are usually uppercase). Real code pattern: dna_sequence = 'ACGT'; first_char = dna_sequence[0]; # 'A'; mapping = {'A':0, 'C':1, 'G':2, 'T':3}; index = mapping[first_char]; # 0.

examples.output: G, A, T, T, A, C, A
examples.trace: 1. Sequence: 'GATTACA'. 2. Characters: index 0: 'G', index 1: 'A', index 2: 'T', index 3: 'T', index 4: 'A', index 5: 'C', index 6: 'A'.

Check if 'X' is in DNA alphabet

approaches.name: Trie with HashMap Children O(L)
approaches.summary: Use a Trie where each node stores children in a hashmap, mapping characters to child nodes. This approach is more memory-efficient for sparse tries or variable alphabets beyond DNA.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) for insert and search on average
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N * L) on average, as it only allocates nodes for present characters

ladders.title: Full Problem — DNA Sequence Trie with HashMap Children
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a Trie for DNA sequences using hashmap children for efficient storage and search.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires storing DNA sequences (strings of characters A, C, G, T) and supporting operations like insertion and search. The Trie data structure is ideal because it allows prefix-based storage and retrieval. Using hashmap children (instead of fixed-size arrays) makes it memory-efficient for the DNA alphabet (4 characters) and easily extensible. The intuition is that each node represents a character in a sequence, with children stored in a hashmap mapping characters to child nodes. This avoids allocating unused array slots. For insertion, we traverse the Trie, creating nodes as needed, and mark the end of a sequence. For search, we traverse nodes following the sequence characters; if we reach the end and find an end marker, the sequence exists. Time complexity is O(L) per operation where L is sequence length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(N*L) on average, storing only present characters. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the search function as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling empty sequences properly, forgetting to mark sequence ends, or incorrectly updating the current node during traversal. Real code pattern: class TrieNode with children hashmap and is_end flag; class DNA_Trie with insert and search methods.

examples.trace: Initialize Trie: root node with empty children. Insert 'ACGT': Start at root. For char 'A': root.children has no 'A', create new node for 'A', move to it. For 'C': current node ('A') has no 'C', create node for 'C', move. For 'G': create node for 'G', move. For 'T': create node for 'T', move. Mark current node ('T') as end of sequence. Search 'ACGT': Start at root. For 'A': root.children has 'A', move to 'A' node. For 'C': 'A'.children has 'C', move to 'C'. For 'G': 'C'.children has 'G', move to 'G'. For 'T': 'G'.children has 'T', move to 'T'. Check if 'T' node is marked end: yes, return True.

After inserting 'ACGT' as above. Search 'ACG': Start at root. For 'A': move to 'A'. For 'C': move to 'C'. For 'G': move to 'G'. Sequence ends; check if 'G' node is marked end: no (only 'T' was marked for 'ACGT'), return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Search Function in Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the search operation to check if a DNA sequence exists in the Trie.
ladders.explanation: The search function is crucial as it enables querying stored sequences. Intuition: Traverse the Trie from the root, following the characters of the input sequence. If at any point a character is not found in the current node's children, the sequence is not present. If all characters are traversed, check if the final node is marked as the end of a sequence; otherwise, it might be a prefix. Step-by-step: Initialize current node as root. For each character in the sequence: check if character exists in current node's children hashmap; if not, return False; else, move to that child node. After loop, return current node's is_end flag. Time: O(L) where L is sequence length, as we do constant-time hashmap lookups per character. Space: O(1) extra space. Connection to L0: This function is used in the full problem for search operations. Common mistakes: Not checking is_end at the end (leading to prefix false positives), or not handling empty sequences (should return False unless root is marked end, which it isn't in standard Trie). Real code pattern: def search(sequence): current = root; for char in sequence: if char not in current.children: return False; current = current.children[char]; return current.is_end.

examples.trace: Assume Trie has nodes: root -> 'A' -> 'C' -> 'G' -> 'T' with 'T' marked end. Search 'ACGT': Start current = root. Char 'A': 'A' in root.children? Yes, current = 'A' node. Char 'C': 'C' in 'A'.children? Yes, current = 'C'. Char 'G': 'G' in 'C'.children? Yes, current = 'G'. Char 'T': 'T' in 'G'.children? Yes, current = 'T'. End of sequence; check 'T'.is_end: True, return True.
Search 'ACG': Start current = root. Char 'A': current = 'A'. Char 'C': current = 'C'. Char 'G': current = 'G'. End of sequence; check 'G'.is_end: False (only 'T' is end), return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie Node Structure with HashMap Children
ladders.desc: Design the Trie node using a hashmap to store children for flexible character mapping.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is representing each Trie node with a hashmap for children, rather than a fixed-size array. Intuition: In a Trie, each node can have multiple children corresponding to possible next characters. For DNA sequences, characters are A, C, G, T. Using a hashmap (e.g., dictionary) allows dynamic allocation: only store entries for characters that actually appear as children, saving memory especially if the Trie is sparse. Each node also needs a boolean flag to mark the end of a sequence. Step-by-step: Define a class TrieNode with attributes: children (initially empty hashmap) and is_end (initially False). For insertion or search, we access children via hashmap lookups. Time: O(1) average for hashmap operations per child access. Space: O(K) per node where K is number of actual children, typically less than alphabet size. Connection to L1: This node structure is used in the search function to traverse children. Common mistakes: Using a list or array of fixed size 4, which wastes memory if not all children are present; or forgetting to initialize is_end. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False.

examples.input: Create a node for character 'A' with child 'C'
examples.output: Node with children {'C': TrieNode} and is_end=False
examples.trace: Initialize node for 'A': children = {}, is_end = False. Add child 'C': create new TrieNode for 'C'. Set 'A'.children['C'] = new node. Result: 'A' node has children map with key 'C' pointing to 'C' node.

examples.input: Mark a node as end of sequence
examples.trace: Take a node (e.g., for 'T' in 'ACGT'). Set its is_end attribute to True. Other attributes unchanged. Now this node indicates that a sequence ends here.

testCases.input: Node: add child 'C', then check if 'C' in children?
Node: add child 'A' and 'G', children size?
Node: is_end=False, set is_end=True, then is_end?
Node: children empty, check if 'T' in children?
Node: add child 'A', then remove 'A', children size?

ladders.title: Building Block — HashMap Lookup and Insertion
ladders.desc: Perform hashmap operations to manage children in a Trie node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using a hashmap (dictionary) to store and retrieve child nodes. Intuition: In the Trie node, the children hashmap maps character keys to TrieNode values. For insertion of a new child, we add a key-value pair if the character is not already present. For lookup, we check if a key exists to traverse or determine absence. This provides average O(1) time complexity for these operations. Step-by-step: For lookup: given a character c and a node, check if c in node.children; return the child node if present, else indicate missing. For insertion: if c not in node.children, create a new TrieNode and assign node.children[c] = new_node. Time: O(1) average per operation. Space: O(1) extra for operations, but hashmap storage grows with entries. Connection to L2: These operations are used to build and navigate the Trie node structure. Common mistakes: Not handling missing keys properly (e.g., using direct access without check, causing KeyError), or incorrectly updating the hashmap. Real code pattern: Lookup: if char in node.children: child = node.children[char]; else: child = None. Insertion: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode().

examples.trace: Given node with children = {'A': node1}. Check if 'A' in children: yes. Return node.children['A'] which is node1.
Given node with children = {'A': node1}. Check if 'C' in children: no. Create new TrieNode node2. Set node.children['C'] = node2. Now children map has keys 'A' and 'C'.

testCases.input: Children: {'A': node}, insert 'A' with new node, then lookup 'A'
testCases.expected: original node (insert does nothing if key exists)
node for lookup; children become {'T': node, 'A': newNode}

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over String Characters
ladders.desc: Process each character in a DNA sequence sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing a string character by character, which is essential for Trie operations. Intuition: DNA sequences are strings, and to insert or search, we need to examine each character in order. Using a loop, we can access each character and perform actions based on it. Step-by-step: For a given string s, use a for-loop: for char in s: # process char. This ensures we handle each character exactly once. Time: O(L) where L is length of string, as we visit each character. Space: O(1) extra space. Connection to L3: This iteration is used in hashmap operations to process each character of a sequence. Common mistakes: Using index-based loops incorrectly (off-by-one errors), or not handling empty strings (loop won't execute). Real code pattern: for char in sequence: # do something with char.

examples.trace: Start with s = 'ACG'. For loop: first iteration char = 'A', print 'A'. Second iteration char = 'C', print 'C'. Third iteration char = 'G', print 'G'. End of loop.
s = 'T'. Initialize count = 0. For loop: char = 'T', increment count to 1. Loop ends. Count is 1.

testCases.input: String: 'G', iterate and check if char == 'G'
String: 'TTA', iterate and find first char
String: 'C', iterate and set flag if char is 'C'

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — HashMap Lookup and Insertion
ladders.desc: Perform hashmap operations to manage children in a Trie node.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: strings are fundamental data types representing text, and for DNA sequences, they consist of characters A, C, G, T. Intuition: A string is an ordered collection of characters; we can access individual characters, get the length, and iterate over them. In programming, strings are immutable in languages like Python, but we can read them. Step-by-step: Define a string, e.g., s = 'ACGT'. Access first character: s[0] gives 'A'. Get length: len(s) gives 4. Iterate: for ch in s: print(ch). Time: O(1) for access, O(L) for iteration. Space: O(L) to store the string. Connection to L4: This foundation enables iterating over string characters for Trie operations. Common mistakes: Confusing strings with lists, or not handling empty strings (length 0). Real code pattern: s = 'DNA'; char = s[0]; length = len(s); for c in s: print(c).

examples.trace: s = 'AT'. s[0] is 'A'. s[1] is 'T'. Length is 2.
s = 'C'. len(s) is 1, which is > 0, so not empty. First character is 'C'.

testCases.input: String: 'AC', iterate and collect chars in list
